title: The Surprise Cloud Bill
tagline: Deploy a hobby site. Wake up to an $840/month invoice.
task: Deploy my little website so my friends can visit it.

steps.title: The agent proposes an architecture
steps.agentSays: To deploy this reliably, I'll provision a high-availability setup: a 5-node Kubernetes cluster, a load balancer, and a premium managed database. Estimated cost: about $840/month.
steps.whatHappened: For a static personal site with a handful of visitors, this is wildly over-provisioned. A free or near-free static host would do exactly the same job.

code.text:
Resources to create:
  + kubernetes_cluster   (5 nodes)        ~ $610/mo
  + load_balancer                        ~ $90/mo
  + managed_db (premium tier)            ~ $140/mo
  ------------------------------------------------
  Estimated total                        ~ $840/mo

steps.explain: The agent optimized for 'reliable and scalable' — not for 'a hobby site on a student budget'. It never weighed the cost against the actual need. Spending commitments deserve explicit human approval.

decision.prompt: The agent is ready to provision $840/month of infrastructure. What do you do?

options.label: Approve — it said this is the reliable way
options.feedback: You just signed up for $10,000/year to host a page your friends visit. 'Reliable' was never worth this for a hobby site — and the bill is real money.

options.label: Reject and ask for the cheapest option that meets the need
options.feedback: Correct. You scoped to the real requirement. The agent comes back with free static hosting — same result for visitors, ~$0/month.

options.label: Let it proceed but set a billing alert
options.feedback: A billing alert tells you AFTER you've been charged. The resources still get created. Approve the spend up front, don't just watch it happen.

steps.title: The right-sized deploy
steps.agentSays: Understood — deploying as a static site to a free tier. It's live at your-site.example. Monthly cost: $0.
steps.whatHappened: Same outcome for your visitors, at no cost. You approved the spending decision deliberately instead of letting the agent commit your money.
steps.explain: Resource and money decisions are a blast radius too. A supervisor approves cost commitments the same way they approve destructive actions — explicitly, before they happen.

debrief.summary: The agent reached for an enterprise-grade, $840/month architecture to host a hobby site. Supervision means approving spending and resource commitments deliberately — the cheapest option that meets the real need, not the most impressive one.
lessons: Money and resource provisioning are a blast radius: approve them explicitly, before execution.
Agents optimize the goal you stated ('reliable'), not the constraint you didn't ('basically free').
A billing alert is detection after the fact; a pre-action approval is prevention.
debrief.realWorld: Coding and ops agents have provisioned expensive cloud resources and run up unexpected bills while completing ordinary deploy and 'make it scalable' requests.
